name: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)
high_means: Pituitary pushing hard; likely underproducing thyroid gland (hypothyroid). When TSH >2 + total cholesterol >200 — assume thyroid output or conversion issue.
low_means: Possible hyperthyroid, or from exogenous T3/T4. TSH can be artificially normal from glucocorticoids, dopamine agonists, high-dose iodine, or exogenous thyroid hormone.
why_it_matters: TSH is a control signal — NOT a king marker. 'TSH is not king.' Never read TSH alone — always run with Free T3, Free T4, rT3. Full thyroid panel required. Standard TSH testing misses majority of functional hypothyroidism.

name: Free T3 (Free Triiodothyronine)
optimal_range: Upper half of reference range; >4 pmol/L; 4–6.2 pmol/L
high_means: Hyperthyroid state or over-replacement. Hyperthyroid men have DHT 8–10x above euthyroid averages.
low_means: Conversion problem or hypothyroid. Low T3 = low DHT factory output regardless of testosterone levels. Drives: high total cholesterol, high LDL, high TG, weird SHBG, low free T, low DHT, higher E2:T ratio, elevated ALT/AST, elevated hsCRP.
why_it_matters: The active thyroid hormone. 'T3 is the throttle for the SRD5A gene.' Drives steroidogenesis, mitochondrial biogenesis, keratin synthesis, anagen phase, fat metabolism, heart/liver/kidney/brain function. ~80% made from T4 conversion in liver/tissues. Most impactful thyroid marker for androgen optimization.

name: Free T4 (Free Thyroxine)
high_means: If TSH low + T4 high = hyperthyroid. If TSH high + T4 high with T3 low = conversion problem (DIO1/DIO2 impaired).
low_means: If TSH high + T4 low = underproducing gland. Fix: iodine, selenium, zinc, iron, copper, calories/protein.
why_it_matters: T4 is mostly a pro-hormone; ~80% converted to T3 in liver/tissues. Ideal: upper half of reference range. Patterns: TSH high + T4 low = gland problem; TSH high + T4 normal/high + T3 low = conversion problem.

name: Reverse T3 (rT3)
optimal_range: Low; T3:rT3 ratio >10 is functional target
high_means: Body deliberately braking metabolism. Causes: chronic stress, calorie deficit, low-carb diet, illness, high T4 doses with poor conversion. rT3 competes with T3 at receptors — tissue-level hypothyroid even with normal TSH/T3. DIO3 (inflammatory/stress deiodinase) produces rT3 from T4.
low_means: Favorable — low rT3 means less competition with active T3 at receptors.
why_it_matters: 'You don't treat a ratio — you use rT3 to explain why a guy with normal T3 still feels hypothyroid.' Elevated rT3 explains chronic fatigue, low DHT, hair loss, poor training recovery despite apparently normal labs. T3:rT3 ratio useful only when clearly collapsed (e.g. <5:1).

name: TPO Antibodies (Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies)
high_means: Hashimoto-type autoimmune thyroid attack. Fix: gut/endotoxin, micronutrients (selenium, zinc, iron, magnesium, D), remove immune triggers (chronic infections, seed oils, heavy alcohol).
low_means: No autoimmune attack present — favorable.
why_it_matters: Autoimmune thyroid destruction is the most common cause of hypothyroidism. Must always run with TgAb for complete autoimmune picture. Positive TPO with positive TgAb = strong Hashimoto indicator.

name: Thyroglobulin Antibodies (TgAb)
high_means: Hashimoto-type autoimmunity. Run alongside TPO antibodies for complete picture.
low_means: No autoimmune thyroid attack — favorable.
why_it_matters: Paired with TPO antibodies to confirm autoimmune thyroid disease. Either or both can be elevated in Hashimoto's.

name: LH (Luteinizing Hormone)
high_means: LH high (7-8+) + T low/mediocre = Leydig cell insensitivity or primary hypogonadism (testicular hardware problem). Check FSH, inhibin B. Consider HCG/enclomiphene.
low_means: LH low + T low = central/functional shutdown (hypothalamic or pituitary). Check TSH/FT3, prolactin, cortisol, iron, hsCRP.
why_it_matters: 'How hard the brain is pushing the balls.' Always read LH with total testosterone. LH pattern determines whether to fix upstream (brain/pituitary) or testicular level. Never read LH alone.

name: FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone)
high_means: High FSH + low sperm = Sertoli cell/testicular damage. Concerning for fertility.
low_means: Low FSH with low LH = central suppression (thyroid, prolactin, stress, medications, obesity, androgens).
why_it_matters: Most clinically relevant at extremes or for fertility evaluation. Always read with LH and testosterone.

name: Total Testosterone
optimal_range: 650–900 ng/dL; most men feel best in this range with good free T and DHT
standard_range: 300–1,000 ng/dL (wide reference range; 'normal' but symptomatic at lower end)
high_means: Above 1,000 ng/dL endogenously: check SHBG, free T, DHT. Very high without symptoms generally favorable. Supraphysiological typically from TRT.
low_means: Below 500 ng/dL with symptoms = problem. Men at 300–400 ng/dL 'feel like trash.' Minimum target >600 ng/dL; functional goal 650–900 ng/dL with good free T.
why_it_matters: Total testosterone alone is insufficient — must read with free T, SHBG, DHT, and E2 for complete picture. 'High total T with trash free T and trash DHT = not impressive.' Synthesized from cholesterol via StAR/CYP11A1/CYP17A1 pathway.

name: Free Testosterone
optimal_range: >=1% of total T; 2–3% is good performance target; >=20 pg/mL
high_means: High free T with high total T generally favorable. High free T with low SHBG — check for insulin resistance or liver issues.
low_means: Total T normal + free T low = binding problem (usually high SHBG). Total T low + free T low = production problem. Only 1–3% truly free; 30–50% albumin-bound (bioavailable).
why_it_matters: Only free (and albumin-bound) testosterone is bioavailable for tissues. SHBG-bound T is essentially inactive. 'High total T is meaningless if SHBG is eating it all.'

name: SHBG (Sex Hormone Binding Globulin)
high_means: SHBG >45–50 = often high thyroid/estrogen/underfueling (low insulin/low carbs/excessive fasting). Binds DHT > T > androstenediol > estradiol. High SHBG eats free testosterone and DHT simultaneously.
low_means: SHBG <15 = often fatty liver/insulin resistance/androgen abuse. Low SHBG means more free androgens but also signals metabolic dysfunction.
why_it_matters: Liver-made protein. Tight binder of DHT and T — key variable between total and free androgen status. Levers to lower high SHBG: eat enough carbs/calories; reduce estradiol; fix liver/gut. Levers to raise low SHBG: fix liver/insulin resistance. Free T:SHBG ratio >=0.3 is a target.

name: DHT (Dihydrotestosterone)
optimal_range: 60–120 ng/dL; 80–120 ng/dL solid if hair/prostate OK
high_means: Chronically very high DHT can trigger AR saturation at hypothalamus -> suppresses LH -> lowers T. Check T:DHT ratio.
low_means: DHT <40–50 ng/dL despite good T = 'under-androgenized at tissue level.' Low DHT causes: low T (no substrate), suppressed 5AR, high SHBG, inflammation, low T3, finasteride/dutasteride, low zinc/NADPH.
why_it_matters: Most potent natural androgen. Binds AR 2–3x stronger than T. Up to 10x more transcriptional potency. Does NOT aromatize to estrogen. T:DHT ratio target ~8–15:1. 'Low DHT isn't just low sex drive. It's low you.' Test separately — total testosterone does not predict DHT.

name: Estradiol / E2 (Sensitive Assay)
high_means: High E2: water retention, emotional instability, gynecomastia risk, high prolactin, erectile dysfunction, insomnia, hair issues. Main causes: excess aromatase from body fat, poor liver clearance, gut recirculation (beta-glucuronidase), seed oils, zinc/B-vitamin deficiency. Lower by: reducing body fat, fixing insulin/leptin, improving sleep, supporting liver/bile/gut.
low_means: Too low E2 (<15): joint pain, flat libido, poor vascular health, cognitive decline, dry skin. Estrogen is necessary — the goal is balance, not elimination.
why_it_matters: Necessary for joints, brain, libido, vascular health — but must be kept in tight range. Excess aromatase (fat mass) is the primary cause. Always run sensitive assay (not standard immunoassay) for males.

name: Prolactin
high_means: Prolactin >10–15 = investigate; >25–30 repeated = proper workup for pituitary tumor. High prolactin: suppresses GnRH -> low LH/FSH; directly inhibits 5AR; lowers T/DHT (~58% DHT drop in study; reversed in 48–72h). Causes: hypothyroidism (TRH stimulates both), high E2, chronic stress/sleep deprivation, obesity/IR, nutritional deficiencies (B6, zinc, magnesium), medications (antipsychotics, antidepressants, opioids, GI drugs), pituitary tumor.
low_means: Low prolactin is not a clinical concern in this context.
why_it_matters: Direct, fast-acting DHT suppressor. Must control prolactin for DHT optimization. Test alongside testosterone, DHT, E2, TSH for complete picture.

name: IGF-1 (Insulin-Like Growth Factor 1)
optimal_range: 160–250 ng/mL for active men 20–40y; upper half of age-adjusted range
high_means: High IGF-1 generally favorable within physiological range. Supraphysiological from GH abuse has health risks.
low_means: IGF-1 <120 = functionally low. Causes: poor sleep (GH pulses during deep sleep), liver dysfunction/inflammation, low insulin/protein/calories, hypothyroidism, low androgens. Low IGF-1 reduces 5AR type 1 activity in skin/muscle.
why_it_matters: GH triggers liver to make IGF-1. Drives muscle/bone/cartilage/tendon, brain, skin/hair, metabolic health, DHT and erectile function. IGF-1 activates 5AR type 1 via PI3K/Akt in skin/muscle.

name: Cortisol (AM Serum)
optimal_range: Mid-range of reference; ~14–18 mcg/dL AM. Healthy DHEA-S:cortisol ratio ~20:1.
high_means: High AM + high PM = overstressed. Normal AM + high PM = 'tired but wired.' High cortisol = muscle breakdown, lower T, higher rT3, more aromatase, raised prolactin, central fat gain, insulin resistance.
low_means: Flat/low = burnout. Low cortisol + low DHEA-S = burnout pattern.
why_it_matters: Single serum test = just a snapshot. Better: 4-point salivary or DUTCH. Cortisol and testosterone compete for cholesterol — 'cortisol goes up, testosterone goes down.' Chronic stress pattern = low testosterone, high aromatase, elevated rT3, suppressed LH.

name: DHEA-S (Dehydroepiandrosterone Sulfate)
optimal_range: Upper half of age-adjusted range; men 20–40y: 300–450 mcg/dL; men 40–60y: 200–350 mcg/dL
high_means: Not clearly stated as concerning in KB at physiological levels.
low_means: Low DHEA-S: adrenal insufficiency, pregnenolone steal from chronic stress, overtraining, inflammation, nutrient gaps (zinc/vitamin C/cholesterol). High cortisol + low DHEA-S = chronic stress catabolic tilt. Low cortisol + low DHEA-S = burnout.
why_it_matters: Adrenal precursor for androgens; neurosteroid; cortisol counterweight. DHEA-S + cortisol pattern interpretation is key. Declines ~2%/year after age 30 — parallels androgenetic alopecia progression. DHEA-S:cortisol ratio may be more important than absolute levels.

name: Fasting Insulin
high_means: Insulin resistance. >10 with normal glucose = likely IR. IR suppresses testosterone, raises SHBG, impairs thyroid conversion, upregulates scalp 5AR independently (local hair DHT problem). TG:HDL >2 often co-presents.
low_means: Favorable at 2–6. Too low (<1) may indicate insufficient food intake.
why_it_matters: 'Insulin is not just a fat storage hormone — needed for muscle preservation, IGF-1, T3, LH/gonadal axis, electrolytes, NO, satiety.' Goal: low fasting, big healthy pulses when eating, fast clearance after. IR is a silent testosterone suppressor that appears in lipids before glucose levels change.

name: HbA1c (Glycated Haemoglobin)
high_means: Average glucose elevated over 90–120 days. Indicates glucose management failure; associated with insulin resistance, aromatase upregulation, low testosterone.
low_means: Very low HbA1c with low average glucose — generally favorable.
why_it_matters: Reflects 90–120 day average glucose. Use with fasting insulin for full insulin sensitivity picture. Pair with fructosamine for shorter-term trends.

name: TG:HDL Ratio (Triglycerides to HDL)
optimal_range: <2 (mg/dL units); ~1 or less = elite insulin sensitivity
standard_range: <2 low risk; 2–3 moderate; >3 high IR probability
high_means: >3 = likely insulin resistance. TG:HDL is a reliable proxy for IR that often appears before glucose/HbA1c becomes abnormal.
low_means: ~1 or less = excellent insulin sensitivity.
why_it_matters: Most reliable lipid-based proxy for insulin sensitivity and metabolic health. If TG:HDL >2 or fasting insulin >10, insulin resistance is likely the hidden bottleneck for testosterone.

name: Triglycerides
high_means: Elevated TG: excess carbohydrate (especially fructose), insulin resistance, alcohol, poor thyroid. High TG + low HDL = classic insulin resistance pattern.
low_means: Very low TG generally favorable.
why_it_matters: High fructose intake directly raises TG via de novo lipogenesis. Part of TG:HDL ratio for IR assessment.

name: HDL Cholesterol
high_means: High HDL generally favorable (but very high HDL may reflect dysfunctional particles — interpret with context).
low_means: Low HDL + high TG = classic insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome. Associated with cardiovascular risk.
why_it_matters: Part of TG:HDL ratio for insulin sensitivity. Essential component of lipid panel interpretation.

name: LDL Cholesterol
optimal_range: <100 mg/dL; interpret with ApoB, hsCRP, context
high_means: Context-dependent. 'High LDL in a lean, insulin-sensitive guy with low ApoB, low hsCRP and a zero CAC scan is not the same risk as high LDL in a diabetic with chronic inflammation.' Fix thyroid first — if thyroid normalizes and LDL stays very high, then look at IR, diet, genetics.
low_means: Very low LDL (<140 total cholesterol) in lifters often correlates with low hormones/T3.
why_it_matters: Never interpret LDL alone — always pair with ApoB, hsCRP, insulin sensitivity markers, thyroid. LDL is often high from hypothyroidism and normalizes with thyroid correction.

name: ApoB (Apolipoprotein B)
high_means: High atherogenic particle count. Better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone. Each LDL and VLDL particle carries one ApoB molecule — counts actual atherogenic particles.
low_means: Favorable cardiovascular risk profile.
why_it_matters: More meaningful than LDL for cardiovascular risk. Interpret with hsCRP and insulin sensitivity.

name: Lp(a) (Lipoprotein a)
high_means: More atherogenic and pro-thrombotic than regular LDL due to attached apolipoprotein(a). Amplifies ApoB risk. 'Lifestyle barely moves it.' Almost entirely genetic. Use as risk multiplier — tighten all other cardiovascular markers.
low_means: Favorable.
why_it_matters: Genetic cardiovascular risk marker. Cannot be meaningfully changed by lifestyle. Context marker rather than intervention target.

name: hsCRP (High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein)
optimal_range: <0.5 mg/L optimal; <1.0 acceptable; <0.3 best for DHT optimization
high_means: 1.0–3.0 = mild chronic inflammation. >3.0 = something driving it (gut, infection, visceral fat, seed oils). Chronic inflammation: blunts GnRH -> lower LH -> lower T; raises SHBG; epigenetically silences SRD5A2 (5AR gene). 'Men with higher hsCRP exhibit significantly lower total and free testosterone, independent of BMI.'
low_means: <0.5 = very clean. Favorable for testosterone, DHT, hair, and metabolic function.
why_it_matters: One of the most impactful hidden suppressors of testosterone and DHT. Inflammation is a direct epigenetic brake on the DHT-making enzyme. hsCRP target: <0.5 for optimization; >1.0 demands investigation of cause.

name: Ferritin
standard_range: 12–300 ng/mL (wide range; lower end differs by lab)
high_means: Ferritin >150 ng/mL with elevated hsCRP: check TSAT — may indicate iron overload. High ferritin + high hsCRP correlates inversely with testosterone (Liu et al., 2013, PLOS ONE). Iron overload -> Leydig cell oxidative damage -> raised SHBG -> lower T. Ferritin is also an acute-phase reactant — rises with inflammation regardless of iron status.
low_means: Ferritin <40–50 ng/mL correlates with hair loss even when hemoglobin is normal. Below 40 = iron deficiency likely impacting follicle metabolism.
why_it_matters: Always interpret with hsCRP. High ferritin is not always 'good iron stores' — it can mean inflammation. Target 50–100 ng/mL for testosterone optimization and hair health.

name: TSAT (Transferrin Saturation)
high_means: 45–50%+ = iron overload pattern. Always check alongside ferritin and hsCRP.
low_means: <20% = likely iron deficiency.
why_it_matters: Paired with ferritin and serum iron for complete iron status picture. Better at identifying true iron deficiency than ferritin alone (which can be falsely elevated by inflammation).

name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
optimal_range: 40–60 ng/mL general; 50–80 ng/mL for athletic focus
high_means: Above 80–100 ng/mL potentially pro-oxidant at very high doses. Above 150 ng/mL = toxicity concern.
low_means: 86% of men with pattern hair loss are vitamin D deficient. Vitamin D deficiency limits Leydig cell STAR protein activation. D3 1-year supplementation raised total T ~25% and free T ~20% in deficient men.
why_it_matters: Always pair with PTH and calcium. VDR (vitamin D receptor) expressed in follicle stem cells. VDR knockout mice develop alopecia. 'You don't judge vitamin D in isolation.'

name: Zinc (Serum)
high_means: Above 130+ mcg/dL without reason can push copper too low. Monitor ceruloplasmin.
low_means: Below 90 mcg/dL = suboptimal. Zinc deficiency -> lower T and DHT (restricts steroidogenic enzymes). 20-week zinc restriction cut testosterone in healthy young men.
why_it_matters: Always read with copper and ceruloplasmin. Long-term high-dose zinc supplementation depletes copper. Zinc is a cofactor for steroidogenic enzymes and immune function.

name: Copper (Serum)
high_means: High copper + low zinc = dysregulated mineral status. Elevated copper without elevation in ceruloplasmin may indicate unbound ('free') copper toxicity.
low_means: Copper deficiency: anemia, neurological symptoms, disrupted dopamine synthesis (dopamine drives LH).
why_it_matters: Always read with ceruloplasmin. Long-term high-dose zinc supplementation depletes copper. Copper required for dopamine synthesis which drives LH (and thus testosterone).

name: Selenium (Serum)
high_means: Above 140 ng/mL = no additional benefit; increases pro-oxidant risk.
low_means: Selenium deficiency -> impaired DIO1 deiodinase (T4->T3 conversion and rT3 clearance) -> functional hypothyroidism -> lower DHT. Also impairs Leydig cell protection.
why_it_matters: Cofactor for all three deiodinase enzymes. Required for thyroid hormone activation. Leydig cell antioxidant. Target 100–130 ng/mL; above 140 gives no additional gain.

name: Magnesium (RBC — Red Blood Cell)
optimal_range: RBC Mg: 5.0–6.5 mg/dL. Serum Mg: 0.85–1 mmol/L.
standard_range: RBC Mg varies by lab; serum Mg 0.75–0.95 mmol/L
high_means: Not clearly a concern at dietary levels.
low_means: Serum magnesium drops late — it is tightly regulated. RBC magnesium is the better measure of true intracellular status. Low RBC Mg: impaired Mg-ATP, sleep disruption, cortisol elevation, steroidogenesis limitation.
why_it_matters: RBC magnesium is more sensitive than serum. Serum drops only after significant depletion. Chronic stress depletes magnesium rapidly. Required for Mg-ATP (all enzyme reactions), nerve/muscle function, GLUT4, sleep.

name: Vitamin B12
high_means: Not clearly stated in KB at dietary supplementation levels.
low_means: 200–300 pg/mL = 'normal' per labs but often correlates with fatigue and neuropathy. Below 400 pg/mL is functionally low for neurological function.
why_it_matters: Required for methylation, neurological function, DNA synthesis. Deficiency presents as fatigue/neuropathy before labs look abnormal. If B12 unclear, pair with MMA (methylmalonic acid) for functional status.

name: Homocysteine
high_means: >10 µmol/L = find cause: B2, B6, folate, B12, choline, or kidney function issue. Elevated homocysteine = methylation cycle disruption and cardiovascular risk.
low_means: Very low homocysteine generally favorable.
why_it_matters: Indicator of B-vitamin methylation cycle function. High homocysteine suggests B2/B6/B12/folate deficiency and increases cardiovascular risk.

name: ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase)
high_means: Liver-specific marker of damage or inflammation. Elevated after hard training (muscle contains some ALT) — do not test within 5–7 days of heavy lifting. High ALT can indicate fatty liver, alcohol use, medications, toxin load.
low_means: Low ALT is favorable.
why_it_matters: ALT is liver-specific (unlike AST which also comes from muscle). Always run with AST, GGT. Do NOT test within 5–7 days of heavy lifting.

name: AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase)
high_means: AST also found in muscle — elevated after hard training is NORMAL. Liver-specific interpretation requires comparison with ALT (ALT:AST ratio).
low_means: Low AST is favorable.
why_it_matters: Run alongside ALT. AST:ALT ratio pattern helps identify source (muscle vs liver). Do not test within 5–7 days of heavy lifting.

name: GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase)
high_means: Sensitive to alcohol and toxic load. High GGT + high ALP = biliary/cholestatic issue. High GGT with normal ALP = alcohol, medications, fatty liver.
low_means: Low GGT is favorable.
why_it_matters: Most sensitive liver enzyme for alcohol and toxin load. Useful for assessing total toxic burden on liver.

name: Albumin
high_means: Not clearly a concern in this context.
low_means: Albumin <4.0 in a young person: ask why — liver disease, malnutrition, inflammation. Albumin also carries bioavailable (weakly bound) testosterone.
why_it_matters: Liver functional capacity marker. Albumin-bound testosterone is bioavailable (unlike SHBG-bound). Low albumin = reduced bioavailable T even if total T looks normal.

name: Creatinine + eGFR
optimal_range: eGFR >=90 mL/min/1.73m2 for young trained male
high_means: High creatinine / low eGFR may indicate kidney impairment — but in trained men, muscle mass elevates creatinine artificially. Use cystatin C for lifters.
low_means: Low creatinine may indicate low muscle mass.
why_it_matters: Use cystatin C for lifters rather than creatinine-based eGFR — less influenced by muscle mass. Track trends over time.

name: Cystatin C
high_means: Kidney function declining. More reliable in trained men than creatinine-based eGFR.
low_means: Favorable kidney function.
why_it_matters: Better kidney marker for athletes/lifters because it is not influenced by muscle mass (unlike creatinine). Trend over time matters more than single value. Monitor when using high-dose creatine.

name: RBC (Red Blood Cell Count)
high_means: Elevated RBC (polycythemia): can indicate dehydration, sleep apnea, or exogenous androgens. Increases blood viscosity.
low_means: Low RBC = anemia. Possible iron, B12, folate deficiency.
why_it_matters: Part of complete blood count. Read with hemoglobin, hematocrit, MCV for anemia pattern identification.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: Hemoglobin (Hgb)
high_means: High Hgb: possible dehydration, polycythemia vera, sleep apnea, exogenous androgens.
low_means: Low Hgb = anemia. Impairs oxygen delivery to all tissues including testes and follicles.
why_it_matters: Oxygen-carrying capacity. Anemia impairs training performance, recovery, and testosterone production.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: MCV (Mean Corpuscular Volume)
high_means: Macrocytosis: B12 or folate deficiency (megaloblastic anemia), alcohol, hypothyroidism. High MCV = large RBCs that do not function optimally.
low_means: Microcytosis: iron deficiency anemia. Small RBCs — iron-deficient state.
why_it_matters: Classifies anemia type: low MCV = iron deficiency; high MCV = B12/folate/alcohol. Guides what deficiency to fix.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: RDW (Red Blood Cell Distribution Width)
high_means: High RDW = mixed populations of RBCs of different sizes. Indicates mixed deficiency (iron + B12/folate simultaneously), inflammation, or chronic disease.
low_means: Normal RDW is favorable.
why_it_matters: Flagging tool for nutritional complexity. High RDW with anemia suggests multiple simultaneous deficiencies.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: WBC with Differential (White Blood Cell Count)
optimal_range: 4.5–9.0 x10^3/µL total WBC; NLR (Neutrophil:Lymphocyte Ratio) <2.5 optimal
high_means: High WBC: active infection, inflammation, or physiological stress. High neutrophils + low lymphocytes = high NLR = chronic inflammatory/stress state.
low_means: Low WBC: viral illness, nutritional deficiency, immune suppression.
why_it_matters: NLR (Neutrophil:Lymphocyte Ratio) = neutrophils divided by lymphocytes. NLR >3.0 = elevated systemic stress/inflammation. NLR is a cheap, calculated inflammatory proxy using CBC data. High NLR correlates with worse outcomes across many conditions.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: Platelets
high_means: High platelets (>400): inflammation, iron deficiency, reactive thrombocytosis after illness/surgery.
low_means: Low platelets: immune destruction, viral illness, liver disease, bone marrow issues.
why_it_matters: Part of standard CBC interpretation. Extreme values warrant further investigation.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, CBC Panel section (Patch v1)

name: DUTCH — Androsterone (5α pathway metabolite)
standard_range: Not clearly stated in KB — compare within DUTCH report ranges
high_means: High androsterone relative to etiocholanolone = strong 5-alpha pathway activity (favorable for DHT).
low_means: Low androsterone with high etiocholanolone = 5-alpha pathway depressed. Pattern: DHT stubbornly low despite solid T, FT3, IGF-1.
why_it_matters: DUTCH urinary androgen metabolites measure 5alpha vs 5beta pathway balance. Androsterone (5alpha-reduced) vs etiocholanolone (5beta-reduced). Low 5alpha:5beta ratio = reduced 5AR activity. Phase 2/3 testing — advanced. Not MVP panel.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, DUTCH Add-on (Patch v1)

name: DUTCH — Etiocholanolone (5beta pathway metabolite)
standard_range: Not clearly stated in KB — compare within DUTCH report ranges
high_means: High etiocholanolone relative to androsterone = 5-beta pathway dominant = reduced 5AR (5-alpha) activity.
low_means: Low etiocholanolone relative to androsterone = favorable 5-alpha dominance.
why_it_matters: 5beta pathway produces androgenic steroids with far less potency than 5alpha pathway. Dominance of 5beta pattern = under-androgenized tissues despite adequate serum testosterone.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, DUTCH Add-on (Patch v1)

name: DUTCH — 3alpha-diol-G (DHT Breakdown Metabolite)
optimal_range: Not clearly stated in KB — lower relative value more favorable
high_means: High 3alpha-diol-G = fast DHT breakdown (via 3alpha-HSD enzyme). DHT is being made but rapidly inactivated.
low_means: Lower 3alpha-diol-G = slower DHT breakdown = longer DHT tissue half-life.
why_it_matters: Identifies whether DHT production OR DHT clearance is the limiting factor. If androsterone is reasonable but 3alpha-diol-G is high = DHT made but rapidly broken down. Apigenin and nicotine (non-smoke) inhibit 3alpha-HSD, slowing this breakdown.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide, DUTCH Add-on (Patch v1)

name: PTH (Parathyroid Hormone)
optimal_range: 20–40 pg/mL when calcium and D are in range
high_means: Chronically elevated PTH -> more bone resorption. Often indicates vitamin D insufficiency pushing PTH up to maintain calcium.
low_means: Very low PTH with high calcium: possible hypoparathyroidism or vitamin D toxicity.
why_it_matters: Always pair with vitamin D and calcium. PTH is the compensatory signal when vitamin D/calcium status is poor.

name: Uric Acid
high_means: High uric acid: insulin resistance and/or high fructose intake. High fructose -> uric acid -> lowers nitric oxide -> worse blood flow/vascular health.
low_means: Very low uric acid not clearly stated as a concern in KB.
why_it_matters: Useful marker for fructose load and insulin resistance. Elevated in IR and metabolic syndrome.

name: Hematocrit (Hct)
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a standalone optimal range in KB; interpreted alongside hemoglobin
high_means: Dehydration, smoking, high altitude, EPO/androgens, sleep apnea
low_means: Anemia, iron/B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, bleeding
why_it_matters: Measures blood concentration / oxygen-carrying capacity. Read alongside hemoglobin — same causes drive both. Context: dehydration, smoking, sleep apnea.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: MCH (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin)
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a standalone optimal range in KB
high_means: Not clearly stated in KB
low_means: Low MCH with low Hb/Hct and low MCV → iron deficiency pattern
why_it_matters: Read as part of the iron-deficiency pattern triad: low MCV + low MCH + low Hb/Hct. Not interpreted in isolation. KB note: 'MCHC not clearly extracted from ebook text — do not create detailed MCHC logic without further source verification.'
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: MCHC (Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration)
high_means: Not clearly stated in KB
low_means: Not clearly stated in KB
why_it_matters: KB patch note: 'MCHC not clearly extracted from ebook text — do not create detailed MCHC logic without further source verification.' Record created for linkability; interpret using standard clinical rules only.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: WBC Total (White Blood Cell Count)
optimal_range: ~4–8 ×10⁹/L rough zone per KB; differential matters more than total alone
high_means: Infection, acute inflammation, stress, hard training/trauma, smoking. Not clearly stated as a standalone interpretation rule in KB — use differential.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a standalone interpretation rule in KB — use differential.
why_it_matters: 'Quick guide gives rough WBC zone ~4–8 ×10⁹/L. Differential matters more than total.' Always read WBC total alongside the neutrophil/lymphocyte breakdown and NLR.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Neutrophils (% of WBC differential)
high_means: Bacterial infection, acute inflammation, recent injury/surgery, cortisol/stress response, hard training/trauma, smoking
low_means: Viral infections, some drugs, B12/folate deficiency, autoimmune neutropenia, marrow suppression issues
why_it_matters: Fast 'kill now' immune cells. Used with lymphocytes to calculate NLR (neutrophils ÷ lymphocytes). NLR target: 1–3:1; >3–4 warrants investigation. 'Use with NLR, hsCRP, symptoms, recent training context.'
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Lymphocytes (% of WBC differential)
high_means: Viral infections, chronic infections, autoimmune activation, lymphoproliferative disease
low_means: Chronic stress/high cortisol, steroids/immunosuppressants, nutrient deficiencies, heavy alcohol
why_it_matters: Adaptive immune cells (viral/memory response). Low lymphocytes with high neutrophils = elevated NLR = stress/inflammation signal. 'Useful stress/cortisol context when low.' NLR = neutrophils ÷ lymphocytes; target 1–3:1.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Monocytes (% of WBC differential)
high_means: Chronic infections, autoimmune disease, recovery phase post-acute infection, blood disorders, chronic inflammation
low_means: Not clearly stated in KB — usually not significant alone
why_it_matters: 'Clean-up crew / chronic stuff.' Treat as pattern marker, not standalone diagnosis. Elevations suggest chronic rather than acute immune activity.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Eosinophils (% of WBC differential)
high_means: Allergies, asthma/atopy, parasites, autoimmune disease, drug reactions, low cortisol/adrenal insufficiency
low_means: Stress/cortisol or steroids; usually not clinically significant alone
why_it_matters: 'Useful for allergy/parasite/histamine context flags.' Not interpreted in isolation. Elevated eosinophils can signal occult parasitic infection or undiagnosed allergy contributing to systemic inflammation.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Basophils (% of WBC differential)
high_means: Chronic allergy/histamine issues, hypothyroidism, rare myeloproliferative disorders
low_means: Common; can go down with steroids/stress/acute infections — usually not significant alone
why_it_matters: 'High value can point to histamine/allergy/thyroid context.' Rarely clinically significant alone; flag for allergy and thyroid workup when elevated.
source_refs: Blood Work Guide (CBC Panel Expansion, Patch v1)

name: Total Cholesterol
optimal_range: 160–200 mg/dL for most lifters; very low (<140 mg/dL) often correlates with low hormones/T3
high_means: When TSH >2 + total cholesterol >200 → assume thyroid output or conversion issue. Elevated with hypothyroidism, liver dysfunction, or dietary excess.
low_means: Very low (<140 mg/dL) often correlates with low hormones/T3 in lifters — cholesterol is the raw material for steroid hormone synthesis
why_it_matters: Cholesterol is the precursor for all steroid hormones (testosterone, DHT, cortisol). 'Very low cholesterol = often low hormones/T3.' Fix thyroid first if LDL/total cholesterol is elevated — hypothyroidism raises cholesterol. Always read with ApoB, hsCRP, context.

name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
high_means: Elevated atherogenic particle burden — calculated as total cholesterol minus HDL; includes LDL + VLDL + IDL
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern at low levels in KB
why_it_matters: 'Non-HDL: aim <130 mg/dL.' Captures all atherogenic cholesterol fractions in one number. Useful when TG is elevated (VLDL inflates LDL calculation errors). Read alongside ApoB.

name: Fasting Glucose
high_means: Insulin resistance, prediabetes, or diabetes. Even mildly elevated fasting glucose (5.1–5.5 mmol/L) with elevated fasting insulin = meaningful metabolic dysfunction.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB at low normal levels
why_it_matters: Pair with fasting insulin, HbA1c, TG:HDL, and C-peptide for full metabolic picture. Fasting glucose alone can be normal while insulin is elevated (compensated insulin resistance).

name: C-Peptide
high_means: Excess pancreatic insulin secretion — insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia
low_means: Pancreatic beta-cell insufficiency or exhaustion
why_it_matters: 'Low fasting insulin + decent C-peptide + low TG:HDL ratio = the insulin sensitivity you actually want.' C-peptide distinguishes whether insulin is being secreted by the pancreas (endogenous) vs given exogenously. Useful for confirming pancreatic function in context of metabolic workup.

name: Fructosamine
high_means: Elevated average blood glucose over the past 2–3 weeks — shorter window than HbA1c
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
why_it_matters: Reflects average glucose over 2–3 weeks vs HbA1c's 90–120 day window. Useful for monitoring short-term dietary changes or interventions. Part of the stubborn fat loss / insulin resistance panel: 'Fasting insulin/C-peptide/HbA1c/Fructosamine' workup.

name: Serum Iron
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a specific optimal range in KB; interpret with TSAT and ferritin
high_means: Iron overload when paired with high TSAT and high ferritin
low_means: Iron deficiency; read with TSAT and ferritin for full picture
why_it_matters: Listed in the KB Phase 1 iron panel: 'Ferritin, serum iron, transferrin, TSAT, sTfR.' Serum iron fluctuates daily — never interpret alone. Must be read with ferritin and TSAT.

name: Transferrin
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a specific optimal range in KB
high_means: Iron deficiency (liver upregulates transferrin to carry more iron)
low_means: Iron overload, liver disease, malnutrition, or inflammatory state
why_it_matters: Listed in the KB Phase 1 iron panel alongside ferritin, serum iron, TSAT, sTfR. Transferrin is the iron transport protein — high transferrin with low saturation = iron deficiency; low transferrin with high saturation = overload.

name: Folate (Serum)
high_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
low_means: Methylation impairment; macrocytic anemia (with high MCV); elevated homocysteine
why_it_matters: Essential for methylation alongside B12 and B6. Low folate → elevated homocysteine. Liver is the highest folate-containing organ — liver consumption supports folate status from food. Required in the KB micronutrient panel.

name: Ceruloplasmin
high_means: Inflammation (ceruloplasmin is an acute-phase reactant — rises with hsCRP); or excess copper
low_means: Copper deficiency; unreliable alone if hsCRP is elevated (may appear normal when copper is depleted if inflamed)
why_it_matters: 'Always read with ceruloplasmin.' Ceruloplasmin is copper's primary transport protein — a more reliable marker of copper status than serum copper alone. Must be interpreted alongside hsCRP since it is an acute-phase reactant. 'Aim ~25–35 mg/dL with low hsCRP.'

name: Calcium (Serum)
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a specific optimal range in KB; always pair with PTH and vitamin D
high_means: Hypercalcemia — can indicate vitamin D toxicity, hyperparathyroidism, or malignancy
low_means: Hypocalcemia — often secondary to vitamin D deficiency or hypoparathyroidism
why_it_matters: 'Always pair with PTH and calcium. You don't judge vitamin D in isolation.' Calcium is listed in the KB bone-mineral panel alongside vitamin D 25-OH, PTH, phosphorus, and albumin. Never interpret calcium without PTH and vitamin D context.

name: ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase)
high_means: ALP high + GGT high = biliary/cholestatic liver issue. ALP high + GGT normal = bone issue (not liver). Isolated ALP elevation: bone turnover, growing adolescent, pregnancy.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
why_it_matters: 'High ALP + high GGT = biliary/cholestatic; high ALP + normal GGT = bone issue.' The GGT co-interpretation rule makes ALP highly specific for the biliary vs bone differentiation. Listed in the KB full liver/kidney panel.

name: Bilirubin (Total)
high_means: Liver dysfunction, hemolysis, or biliary obstruction. Isolated mild elevation = Gilbert's syndrome (benign). Elevated bilirubin + rising INR + low albumin = advanced liver disease territory.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
why_it_matters: 'Bilirubin: <1.0 mg/dL; isolated mild elevation (Gilbert's syndrome = benign).' Read alongside INR, albumin, ALT/AST/GGT for liver synthetic function context. Gilbert's syndrome is the most common benign cause of mildly elevated bilirubin.

name: INR (International Normalized Ratio)
high_means: Rising INR = liver losing functional (synthetic) capacity — impaired clotting factor production. INR trending up with prolonged course + high liver enzymes → more advanced liver disease territory.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
why_it_matters: 'Rising INR = liver losing functional capacity.' Use with platelets, albumin, bilirubin, and liver enzymes to assess liver synthetic function as a whole. Not relevant to individuals on anticoagulation therapy in this context.

name: BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)
optimal_range: Not clearly stated as a specific optimal range in KB
high_means: Impaired kidney filtration, dehydration, or high protein intake. High BUN + high creatinine = likely renal impairment.
low_means: Not clearly stated as a concern in KB
why_it_matters: Listed in the KB Phase 1 comprehensive panel: 'BUN, electrolytes, CBC with differential.' BUN/creatinine ratio helps distinguish pre-renal (dehydration) from intrinsic renal causes. Always read with creatinine, eGFR, and cystatin C.

name: Urine ACR (Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio)
high_means: 30–300 = microalbuminuria (early kidney damage signal); >300 = significant/overt damage
low_means: Not a concern
why_it_matters: 'Urine ACR: <30 mg/g normal; 30–300 = microalbuminuria (early damage); >300 = significant damage.' Microalbuminuria is an early marker of kidney stress, endothelial dysfunction, and cardiovascular risk — detectable before eGFR falls.

name: MMA (Methylmalonic Acid)
high_means: Functional B12 deficiency at tissue level even when serum B12 appears normal
low_means: Not a concern
why_it_matters: Confirmatory test for B12 deficiency when serum B12 is borderline (200–400 pg/mL). Elevated MMA with borderline serum B12 = true functional deficiency. Listed in the KB micronutrient panel: 'MMA (if B12 unclear).'
